title: Crawl Graph, Frontier State Machine, Scheduling Identity, and Invariants · Diagnostic Repair Bay
summary: A preserved TypeScript crawl trace contains one plausible scope, URL, robots, Fetch, stream, Cheerio, selector, frontier, Promise, browser, axe, report, security, or shutdown defect; identify the first invalid transition before editing. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in deterministic frontier transition model and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evide…
objectives: Define discovered, rejected, queued, claimed, fetching, fetched, parsed, failed, retryable, terminal, and canceled transitions.
Atomically reserve admitted URL identity when scheduling so concurrent discovery cannot duplicate effects.
Prioritize owner commitments, depth, page classes, freshness, origin fairness, and retries with stable tie rules.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug State Invariants

content.text: A debug TypeScript team handles case 5665 during a hostile redirect, stream, and checkpoint drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove legal frontier states, reservation timing, stable priorities, graph evidence, and invariants; owner authorization and revocation must remain separate from robots and legal-review claims; then change one owner permission, seed, URL, robots, sitemap, redirect, DNS, media, or encoding decision and reconcile provenance. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Check unique active ownership, bounded work, legal transitions, terminal immutability, count reconciliation, and recoverability.

Another successful State Invariants run of the same deterministic frontier transition model happy path. That claim fails the state invariants · fault isolation evidence boundary for State Invariants.

For state invariants · fault isolation, fetch one convenient page, omit owner authorization, source and emitted revision, URL and robots policy, runtime admission, stream and Promise ownership, Cheerio and browser differences, accessibility states, changed fixtures, recovery, and controlled Node transfer, then accept plausible objects as TypeScript crawler proof. This does not satisfy State Invariants for deterministic frontier transition model.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Implement the cause-level repair · Debug State Invariants
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-TypeScript function headed "// Evidence: crawler-ts-state-invariants-1r0pkf" that uses case 3231 to prove legal frontier states, reservation timing, stable priorities, graph evidence, and invariants. Keep defaults runnable and return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import Node APIs, open streams or sockets, resolve DNS, parse through Cheerio, launch Playwright or axe-core, access files or packages, retain credentials, leave handles, or claim native runtime, accessibility, privacy, legal, load, container, restore, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Node 24.18.0, TypeScript 7.0.2 and the TypeScript 6.0.2 compatibility compiler API, Cheerio 1.2.0, robots-parser 3.0.1, p-limit 7.3.0, csv-stringify 6.8.1, Playwright 1.61.1, axe-core 4.12.1, original fixtures, loopback servers, emitted-runtime and handle tests, controlled DNS and browser environments, accessibility review, packages, containers, capacity, backup, restore, rollback, legal review, and release gates.
